macro_segments.title: The Case of Kiddushei Ketanah and the Obligation of Yibum: Introduction and the Debate Between Rav and Shmuel

micro_segments.title: Setting up the scenario: A man married to a minor, his brother dies, and the question of the sister-in-law's status
Rabban Gamliel's position: Teaching the minor girl mi'un or waiting until she matures
The problem: When did the mitzvah of yibum begin, and was the woman an ervah at that time?
Rav Elazar's question to Rav: What is Rabban Gamliel's reasoning?
First possibility: The kiddushin of the ketanah are suspended and take effect automatically when she matures
Second possibility: The original kiddushin are not binding; a new kiddushin with the adult woman takes effect
Rav Sheshet's objection: Rav must have been sleeping when he said this
The baraita: Kiddushei ketanah are suspended (teluyim)
Rav Avin bar Rav Nachman's response: The kiddushin are in abeyance; they only take effect if there is intercourse
The woman's perspective: She has rights just as the man does; she may withhold consent
The core question: Is kiddushei ketanah automatic or contingent on intercourse?

macro_segments.title: The Debate of Rav vs. Shmuel and Rav Sheshet on the Status of Kiddushei Ketanah Upon Maturity

micro_segments.title: Rav's position: A new act of bi'ah (intercourse) is required to effectuate the kiddushin
Challenging Rav's position: A case of a ketanah who grew up without mi'un and accepted kiddushin from another man
Rav's view implied: She is already mekudeshet to the first husband and does not need a get from the second
Gemara's resolution: The case involves the scenario where she had intercourse with the first husband
Shmuel's position: All intercourse is framed by the original kiddushin; a new act of kiddushin is not created
Three-way debate: Automatic marriage (Rav Sheshet), new bi'ah (Rav), or continuation of original (Shmuel)
Connection to Rav Moshe Feinstein's approach to agunah and mamzerut cases
Rav Yosef Eliyahu Henkin's disagreement: The reality of marital intercourse creates kiddushin

macro_segments.title: Application of the Rav-Shmuel Debate to the Case of Kiddushin B'Tnai (Conditional Marriage) and the Limit of Kiddushei Derabbanan

micro_segments.title: Another case where the debate applies: Kiddushin with a tnai followed by living together without reiterating the tnai
Rav's position: The intercourse overrides the tnai and a new kiddushin is effected
Shmuel's position: The intercourse is defined by the context of the original kiddushin and tnai
Gemara's reasoning: In the tnai case, the tnai is explicit and he is aware; in the ketanah case, it is not
Therefore, Rav might agree with Shmuel in the ketanah case where no explicit problem confronts him

macro_segments.title: Challenge to Rav's Position and the Case of the Chariot (Bei Korsaia) in Narash

micro_segments.title: The objection: Did Rav really hold that only with new bi'ah is the ketanah now a di'oraita wife?
The story: A girl married as a ketanah grew up and was being brought to the chuppah when another man abducted her
The ruling: Rav Huna and Rav Chananel (students of Rav) said she does not need a get from the second man
Implication: The kiddushin automatically took effect when she became an adult, without intercourse
Rav Papa's explanation: In Narash, they have intercourse before the chariot ride, so the case involves bi'ah
Rav Ashi's approach: The kiddushin of the second man did not work because he acted improperly (shelo k'hogen)
The principle of hafka'as kiddushin: The Rabbis can overturn kiddushin if the man acts improperly

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Hafka'as Kiddushin: Conditions, Application, and the Distinction Between Ad Hoc and Categorical Cases

micro_segments.title: The issue: Hafka'as kiddushin is case-specific and does not rely on the principle kol mekadesh ada'ata d'rabbanan

macro_segments.title: The Ruling and Its Implications: When the Kiddushin of the Ketanah Automatically Takes Effect

micro_segments.title: Reiteration: The halacha is k'Rabbi Eliezer for teaching the ketanah mi'un

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Hafka'as Kiddushin: Conditions, Application, and the Distinction Between Ad Hoc and Categorical Cases (continued)

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's doubt: Is hafka'as kiddushin based on the ada'ata principle or on the broader power to overturn Torah law?
The distinction: Most hafka'as kiddushin cases involve defined rules within the rabbinic system; this case involves shelo k'hogen
Two categories of hafka'as kiddushin: Categorical (within the system) and ad hoc (outside the system)
The principle yesh koach b'yad chachamim: The Rabbis can override Torah law for improper conduct
Application: The Rabbis refused to recognize the second kiddushin because the man violated ethical norms
Conclusion: The only case of hafka'as kiddushin not based on ada'ata is this shelo k'hogen case

macro_segments.title: The Ruling and Its Implications: When the Kiddushin of the Ketanah Automatically Takes Effect (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef rules: The halacha follows Rabbi Eliezer that the ketanah should be taught to do mi'un
Transition to the next mishnah: A man married to two minor orphans who are not sisters

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Two Wives with Different Status: Ketanah vs. Choreshet, and Which Actions Release the Co-Wife

micro_segments.title: The case: A man married to two minor orphans; if one is divorced or undergoes yibum, the other is released
Similarly with two deaf-mutes (chereshot): They have the same status, so acting on one releases the other
Different status case: One pikachat (adult/hearing) and one choreshet (deaf-mute)
The rule: Bi'ah or chalitzah of the pikachat releases the choreshet, but not vice versa
Reason: One is d'oraita, the other is d'rabbanan; the d'rabbanan cannot impact the d'oraita zikah
Similarly with gedolah and ketanah: The adult releases the minor, but not vice versa
Doubt in the mishnah: How can one do chalitzah with a choreshet?

macro_segments.title: Can a Choreshet (Deaf-Mute Woman) Perform Chalitzah? The Status of Her Marriage and the Limit of Rabbinic Kiddushin

micro_segments.title: The gemara's question: How can the mishnah say chalitzah is possible with a choreshet?
A choreshet is not a bat chalitzah; chalitzah requires specific conditions not met by a deaf-mute
The baraita: Chalitzah performed by or on a cheresh, or by a choreshet, or with a katan is invalid
Rav Gidel's resolution: The mishnah meant only bi'ah in the two chereshot case, not chalitzah
Rava's position: Chalitzah could work if the woman married as a choreshet, so all her obligations are d'rabbanan
The distinction: Choreshet me'ikara (always deaf) vs. pikachat she became choreshet (became deaf)
If always a choreshet: She can do chalitzah just as she could enter marriage, both d'rabbanan
If became deaf: She cannot do chalitzah because the original marriage was d'oraita and requires d'oraita chalitzah

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Challenge: The Mishnah's Cases Appear to Involve Choreshet Me'ikara, Yet Chalitzah Is Not Permitted

micro_segments.title: Abaye challenges Rava's reasoning: Can a choreshet who married as a choreshet really do chalitzah?
The mishnah: Two brothers, one hearing and one deaf, married to two unrelated women, one hearing and one deaf
If the deaf brother (who married the deaf woman) dies: The hearing brother must do yibum but cannot do chalitzah
The question: How can chalitzah not be an option if the marriage was only d'rabbanan (deaf husband with deaf wife)?
Abaye's attempted resolution: Perhaps the woman became deaf after originally marrying as hearing
If the hearing brother dies: The deaf brother can do yibum but cannot give a get to divorce his hearing wife
The logic: He can divorce his wife only if he married her as a deaf man; therefore he was always deaf
Therefore, the deaf woman in the mishnah must have always been deaf, yet chalitzah is not permitted

macro_segments.title: Further Challenges with Sisters: When One Brother Is Deaf and the Other Is Hearing, Both Married to Sisters

micro_segments.title: The scenario: Two hearing brothers married to two sisters; one sister is hearing, one is deaf
If the hearing brother dies: The hearing co-wife's sister cannot do yibum because she is an ervah (acho ishah)
If the deaf brother dies: The deaf co-wife's sister cannot do chalitzah because she is deaf
The problem: He cannot do yibum (acho ishah) or chalitzah (she is deaf); he can only divorce the hearing wife
The result: The deaf sister-in-law becomes an agunah; she is permanently trapped in a d'rabbanan marriage

macro_segments.title: Resolution Attempts: The Mishnah Cases Must Involve Only Those Always in Their Stated Status, Yet Chalitzah Remains Forbidden

micro_segments.title: Proof: When the deaf brother divorces his hearing wife, he must have married her as a deaf man
Therefore: The deaf woman in the mishnah must have been deaf from the beginning
Yet: Even though she married as a deaf woman (d'rabbanan marriage), she cannot perform chalitzah
The evidence: The mishnah explicitly states koneis, ein choletz lo—he does yibum, she does not do chalitzah
Gemara remains silent: No satisfactory answer is found; the gemara defers to the next daf
The conclusion: A choreshet cannot do chalitzah even if she married as a choreshet with only d'rabbanan status

topical_tags.term: Chalitzah (removal of the shoe to release from yibum)
Rav Sheshet (opposing Rav Rav on teluyim)
Kol d'mekadesh ada'ata d'rabbanan mekadesh (intention within rabbinic framework)
Rabban Gamliel (Tanna on kiddushei ketanah resolution)
Rav Avin bar Rav Nachman (Amora on teluyim)
Rav Papa (Amora on the bei korsaia story)
Ad hoc vs. categorical hafka'as kiddushin
Yesh koach b'yad chachamim (Rabbis' power to override Torah)
Achot gerushato (sister of his divorced wife)
